frameworks.name: computational decision-making model of escape behavior
frameworks.one_line: captures latent stimulus-driven bias influencing decisions to escape aversive noise

frameworks.name: stimulus-driven escape / aversion framework
frameworks.one_line: frames suicide-related behavior as escape from acute distress

core_question: Does past suicide attempt relate to a weaker decision bias to actively escape from suicide-related stimuli?
key_assumptions: explicit suicide aversion ratings index motivational valence
latent decision bias is recoverable from escape-task choices via computational modeling
personalized suicide imagery evokes relevant motivational responses
manipulations: stimulus type (personalized suicide pictures vs positive contrasts)
escape decisions tied to acutely distressing noise
key_findings: past-attempt individuals showed much lower explicit suicide aversion than others
suicidal group made more impulsive escape decisions for suicide vs positive stimuli
computational model captured a latent bias driven by suicide stimuli
weaker biases mediated the link between lower suicide aversion and past attempt
interpretations: weaker active-escape bias from suicide stimuli is a novel, specific, incrementally valid correlate of suicide attempt
decision science can illuminate mechanisms of escape-related risk processes in suicidality
